segments.title: Royal Navy decline through risk and threat management
segments.summary: The lecture frames recent Royal Navy decline as a policy habit of treating reduced threats as reduced risks, leaving gaps in escorts, readiness, and trade protection.
segments.body: The discussion captures the episode's organizing argument. Dr. Clarke distinguishes threats, which are visible and politically easier to fund against, from persistent maritime risks such as dependence on global trade and the need to have ships ready before a crisis appears. He links that confusion to shrinking escort numbers, assumptions that two assets can cover a standing role, and maintenance or dry-dock pressure when reality does not match forecasts.

evidence.note: The framing section defines the episode around risk versus threat management and uses maritime trade protection as an example of a persistent risk.
The transcript links reduced forces and escort cuts to assumptions that small numbers of assets can cover roles if nothing unexpected happens.
The end-of-history discussion says the obvious threat declined after the Soviet Union, but the underlying risk remained.
